one_line_takeaway: Splitting a harmful image into fragments (optimally three roughly equal pieces) bypasses VLM safety alignment because RLHF/DPO training only ever saw holistic images, and the authors' Adv-KD-powered Transfer-SIVA attack turns this into a practical, fully black-box jailbreak that boosts cross-model transfer ASR by 39-60% over prior optimization-based attacks, though their proposed augmented-DPO defense only partially mitigates it at small training scale.
key_finding: Naive split-image inputs alone substantially raise ASR over single-image jailbreaks across all three tested models and three datasets, with the 1:1:1 split consistently the strongest ratio (e.g., ASR of 79.6% for Pixtral / 62.9% for Llama / 50.5% for Qwen on JailbreakV at 1:1:1, versus 28.8/17.8/15.5% respectively for the single-image baseline); a proposed lightweight visual-filtering defense (VT module) reduces ASR by up to 52% for Llama-3.2-Vision on the 1:1:1 case but is imperfect (3.75% misclassification rate lets some split attacks through). Adaptive-SIVA, designed to evade this filter, reaches up to ~95% of the naive attack's (filter-free) ASR upper bound while remaining undetected by the VT module, and clearly outperforms three existing single-image optimization/typography baselines (Jailbreak in Pieces, UMK, MML) benchmarked on JailbreakV-28K. White-box Adaptive-SIVA, however, transfers poorly across architecturally different target models (consistent with prior optimization-based attacks). Transfer-SIVA's Adv-KD closes much of this gap: combined RF-DPO+PCL alignment reaches above 80% cosine similarity between surrogate and target adapter embeddings with just 500 distillation samples, and across nearly all black-box transfer combinations of the three models, Adv-KD boosts ASR by 39-60% over the non-KD transfer baseline (matching the abstract's headline '~60% higher transfer success' claim); the same Adv-KD technique also improves the single-image Jailbreak-in-Pieces baseline's transferability by up to 33%, though its absolute ASR stays low due to that attack's inherent single-image weakness. Both tested existing inference-time defenses (Llama Guard 3 Vision, ECSO) fail against Transfer-SIVA. The proposed aDPO mitigation, even trained at small scale (1,000 labeled instances), reduces ASR by up to ~16% for black-box transfer attacks and up to ~9.5% for white-box attacks across all three models.
core_contribution: The paper identifies a previously unexplored vulnerability in the safety alignment of modern Vision-Language Models (VLMs): when a harmful image is split into multiple fragments and fed to the model as separate image inputs, the model's utility pipeline (pretraining/instruction-SFT) correctly reassembles and understands the combined content, but the safety-alignment stage (RLHF/DPO) was trained only on holistic images and fails to recognize harmfulness distributed across fragments, causing the model to comply with harmful requests it would otherwise refuse. Building on this root-cause finding, the authors design a three-phase attack family, Split-Image Visual Jailbreak Attacks (SIVA): (1) Naive-SIVA, simple image splitting; (2) Adaptive-SIVA, a white-box, pair-wise embedding-optimization attack (via PGD in the vision-adapter latent space, with a novel 'strategic seed selection' using an iterative generative-editing loop) that evades a proposed lightweight visual-filtering defense while still exploiting the split-image vulnerability; and (3) Transfer-SIVA, a fully black-box attack that uses a novel Adversarial Knowledge Distillation (Adv-KD) algorithm -- combining a reference-free DPO loss and a proxy contrastive-learning loss -- to align the attacker's surrogate VLM's vision encoder/adapter latent space with an unseen black-box target VLM, enabling the white-box Adaptive-SIVA optimization to transfer across architecturally dissimilar models. The paper also formalizes 14 prior VLM jailbreak methods plus SIVA into a unified mathematical framework (visual/textual objectives and feasible spaces) and proposes a mitigation, augmented DPO (aDPO), that reuses existing preference labels across synthetically split-image variants of each training instance to harden safety alignment without new human annotation.
threat_model: Attacker with a single image-plus-text jailbreak goal against a target VLM in three escalating settings: Phase 1 (Naive-SIVA) is black-box with no visual filtering -- the attacker only splits the image, no gradient access needed. Phase 2 (Adaptive-SIVA) is white-box with a visual-filtering defense in place -- the attacker has gradient/embedding access to the target VLM's vision encoder and adapter to optimize adversarial seed images via PGD (perturbation budget epsilon=16/255, step size alpha=2/255, up to T=200 steps). Phase 3 (Transfer-SIVA) is fully black-box with visual filtering present -- the attacker has only query access to the target model and instead uses a separate, locally-controlled surrogate ('attack') VLM, aligning its latent space to the target's via Adv-KD using solely image-only queries (no text) and the target's generated text responses. Text queries throughout are benign/direct rather than adversarially engineered, since the paper's threat model isolates the image channel as the attack surface.
limitations: Naive-SIVA is trivially defeated by the VT merging defense the authors themselves introduce, motivating the escalation to Phase 2. Adaptive-SIVA requires white-box gradient access to the target model's vision encoder/adapter, an assumption the authors call 'not very practical' for real deployments, and it transfers poorly across models with different ViT architectures/priors (Qwen3-VL-VisionModel, Mllama-VisionModel, Pixtral-VisionModel). Even with Adv-KD, Transfer-SIVA's absolute ASR still varies substantially by target model (Qwen-3-VL consistently shows the strongest inherent safety robustness; Pixtral the weakest) and the paper notes JailbreakV shows slightly higher optimization drift/lower ASR because its bottom-caption typography is harder to reconstruct via embedding alignment. aDPO is only validated at small scale (1,000 human-labeled seed instances) due to resource constraints, and the authors explicitly state larger-scale preference optimization is left to future work rather than demonstrated. The Forbidden Recipe dataset is small (75 images across 4 categories). Evaluation relies on an LLM-judge (GPT-5.1) for ASR scoring, cross-validated only partially against human annotation (details deferred to an appendix not fully retrievable in this extraction).

datasets_used: JailbreakV-28k (1000 randomly sampled instances from the ~2k-instance benchmark; Stable Diffusion-generated images with bottom captions, 16 categories)

Forbidden Recipe (FR) -- newly curated by the authors: 75 real-world images of illegal/forbidden objects across 4 categories (explosives, prohibited drugs, banned tools, weapons), paired with a generic step-by-step-instruction text query
Reddit-NSFW -- newly curated by the authors: 300 explicit/violent images from Reddit posts and comments, used for toxic-caption-generation attack objective
MM-RLHF dataset (1,000 human-labeled safety preference instances used to train the aDPO defense)
quantitative_results: Naive-SIVA ASR at 1:1:1 split vs. single-image baseline on JailbreakV: Qwen 50.5% vs 15.5%, Llama 62.9% vs 17.8%, Pixtral 79.6% vs 28.8%
Naive-SIVA ASR at 1:1:1 split on Forbidden Recipe: Qwen 54.6%, Llama 70.6%, Pixtral 89.3% (vs single-image 13.3/15.3/24.6%)
Naive-SIVA ASR at 1:1:1 split on Reddit-NSFW: Qwen 55.0%, Llama 67.0%, Pixtral 78.6% (vs single-image 15.6/14.0/19.3%)
1:1:1:1 (quadruple) split collapses ASR to near-zero (e.g., 0.0-9.6% across datasets/models) due to loss of model comprehension
VT visual-filtering defense reduces Naive-SIVA ASR by up to 52% for Llama-3.2-Vision on the 1:1:1 case; VT module misclassifies split images as distinct in 3.75% of cases

Adaptive-SIVA reaches up to ~95% of the naive (filter-free) ASR upper bound while evading the VT filter, e.g., 1:1:1 JailbreakV Adaptive ASR: Qwen 47.7%, Llama 60.5%, Pixtral 71.5% vs Naive upper bound 53.5/66.9/79.6%
PGD optimization for Adaptive-SIVA: epsilon=16/255, step size alpha=2/255, max T=200 steps, cosine-similarity loss threshold tau=0.05 typically reached in 70-95 steps

Benchmark study on JailbreakV: MML attack effective only against Pixtral (~43% ASR), ineffective against Qwen-3 and Llama-3.2-Vision; Shayegani et al. (Jailbreak in Pieces) and MML achieve the lowest ASR of all compared single-image attacks; UMK shows slightly better ASR than MML/Shayegani due to added GCG-style text-suffix optimization
Adv-KD latent alignment: RF-DPO+PCL combined achieves above 80% cosine similarity between surrogate and target adapter embeddings using just 500 distillation samples (image-only, no text query)
Transfer-SIVA (with Adv-KD) achieves a 39-60% ASR boost over the non-KD transfer baseline across nearly all 3x3 target/attack model combinations on JailbreakV
Applying Adv-KD to the single-image Jailbreak-in-Pieces baseline increases its transfer ASR by up to 33%, though absolute ASR remains low
aDPO defense reduces ASR by up to ~16% for black-box transfer attacks and up to ~9.5% for white-box (diagonal) attacks, evaluated across all three target models
aDPO trained with K=3 max splits, on roughly 1x10^4 to 8x10^4 augmented preference instances derived from 1,000 human-labeled MM-RLHF seed instances
baselines_compared: Single-image jailbreak baseline (unsplit image, same models/datasets)
FigStep, MM-SafetyBench, Multi-modal linkage (MML) -- typography-based single-image attacks
Visual Adversarial Examples (Qi et al. 2024), imgJP, Carlini et al. 2023, HADES -- gradient/PGD-based single-image optimization attacks
Universal Master Key (UMK), Wang et al. 2025 (Align is not Enough), Bi-modal Adversarial Prompt (BAP) -- joint image+text optimization attacks
Arondight, Safety Snowball Agent (SSA) -- RL-guided / multi-turn escalation attacks
Con Instruction, Jailbreak in Pieces (Shayegani et al.) -- embedding-alignment attacks (closest prior art to SIVA, but single/holistic-image only)
Llama Guard 3 Vision and ECSO -- inference-time defenses tested against Transfer-SIVA and shown to fail
